splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on the timing constraints for actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability under ERISA. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing of assumption selection directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be selected and fixed by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

circuits.name: Sixth Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises questions about limits on judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, allowing courts to revoke supervised release even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling the term of supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and the term continues to run regardless.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: The circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement when bringing successive collateral attacks. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are categorically barred from raising any previously presented claim, in addition to Section 2255(h) requirements. If not, they need only satisfy 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to Section 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under Section 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners filing successive Section 2255 motions.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to Section 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar does not apply to federal prisoners, who need only satisfy Section 2255(h)'s requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. If standing is broadly granted, it becomes far easier to challenge mail-in voting rules, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices nationwide. If denied pre-election, these disputes are funneled into post-election litigation.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates for federal office have Article III standing to challenge state election laws that allegedly dilute or devalue their votes or affect the competitive landscape before an election.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to state ballot-counting laws absent a concrete, particularized injury distinct from generalized grievances.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous provisions in the Sentencing Guidelines that carry criminal consequences.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether courts should defer to the Sentencing Commission's interpretation of ambiguous Guidelines provisions or instead apply the rule of lenity in favor of the defendant. This split has deepened after Loper Bright overruled Chevron and raises fundamental questions about expanding criminal liability through delegated interpretive authority.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from using delegated power to expand criminal liability.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference applies over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions, deferring to the Commission's anti-defendant interpretation.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: The circuits are deeply split on whether a defendant must meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1) to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. This split leads to dramatically disparate sentencing outcomes depending on the circuit, with identical defendants receiving different mandatory minimum sentences.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its natural conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' based on statutory context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference when the guideline text does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses is a permissible interpretation entitled to Stinson/Kisor deference, or whether it impermissibly expands the plain text of the guideline. This split affects whether defendants with prior conspiracy convictions qualify as career offenders.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of inchoate offenses is a reasonable interpretation of an ambiguous guideline entitled to controlling weight under Stinson and Kisor deference.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition beyond what the text allows, so it is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the First Step Act's non-retroactive anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c) can constitute an 'extraordinary and compelling reason' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A), either alone or in combination with other factors.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law — particularly the First Step Act's elimination of mandatory consecutive 'stacked' § 924(c) sentences — can be considered as part of the extraordinary-and-compelling-reasons analysis for compassionate release. This affects prisoners serving extremely long sentences under the old stacking regime who have no other avenue for relief.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law, including the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment, can be considered as one factor contributing to extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedule criminalizes substances not listed on the federal Controlled Substances Act schedules.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the substance underlying a state conviction to also be federally controlled. This split determines whether defendants with state marijuana or other drug convictions qualify for career offender or enhanced base offense level treatment.

positions.label: State schedule controls — no federal match required
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines broadly define 'controlled substance offense' to include any offense under state law, regardless of whether the particular substance is also controlled under federal law.

positions.label: Federal schedule controls — substance must be federally listed
positions.summary: Holds that a state conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance at issue is also on the federal controlled substances schedules, applying a categorical approach.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly punish a defendant for the violation of supervised release conditions at revocation, or whether revocation sentencing must be limited to the goals of supervised release itself.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether revocation sentences may be based on punishment for the violation conduct or must be focused solely on the purposes of supervised release (such as deterrence and rehabilitation). The Sixth Circuit allows explicit punishment at revocation, placing it at an extreme of the split, while other circuits limit the permissible considerations.

positions.label: Courts may punish at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly consider punishment for the violation conduct when imposing a sentence upon revocation of supervised release.

positions.label: Courts may not punish at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences must be based on the breach of trust and the need to promote the purposes of supervised release, not punishment for the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between a Chapter 13 filing and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or the debtor. This split affects debtors' ability to retain home equity gains during reorganization attempts that ultimately fail.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in equity inure to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion, based on the broad scope of 'property of the estate' under § 541(a).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) limits the converted estate to the property as it existed at the petition date, meaning post-petition appreciation in equity belongs to the debtor, not the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal deadline for filing a notice of appeal applies to an order on a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case is governed by the 30-day civil appeal deadline or the 14-day criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises in a criminal case and is governed by the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents referencing private medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The circuits have not uniformly addressed whether denial of a motion to seal medical information is immediately appealable. The D.C. Circuit held that maintaining privacy in medical treatments is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate appellate review, potentially creating tension with circuits that take a narrower view of the collateral order doctrine for sealing orders.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denials of motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' effectively unreviewable after final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are not immediately appealable because post-judgment remedies such as mandamus or appeals from final judgment adequately protect privacy interests.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether claims that might also be raised in habeas proceedings (such as legal errors in the underlying conviction) can form part of the compassionate release analysis. Some circuits permit courts to consider such factors, while others hold that compassionate release cannot serve as a substitute for habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider potential legal errors or unfairness in the underlying conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release cannot be used to address legal errors in the conviction or sentence, as those claims must be brought through habeas proceedings under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether state tort claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting or monitoring motor carriers are preempted by the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to' the price, route, or service of a motor carrier or broker. This affects liability for trucking accidents arranged through brokers nationwide.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they are 'related to' the broker's services within the meaning of the FAAAA preemption clause.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA's preemption provision does not extend to state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or selection of motor carriers, as such claims do not regulate the broker's price, route, or service.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal prohibition on firearm possession by unlawful users of controlled substances, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen, circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by unlawful drug users is constitutional as applied to marijuana users. Some circuits have upheld the statute, while others have found it unconstitutional, creating divergent rules on gun rights for marijuana users across the country.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because historical tradition supports disarming those who use intoxicating substances.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming those who use controlled substances.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label. This affects products liability litigation involving herbicides and pesticides nationwide, including high-profile Roundup litigation.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they parallel, rather than impose requirements 'in addition to or different from,' federal labeling requirements.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, exempting them from mandatory arbitration.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to construe the FAA's Section 1 exemption for transportation workers. The question of whether last-mile delivery workers who distribute goods to retail stores are 'transportation workers' engaged in interstate commerce has divided the circuits, with implications for millions of workers in logistics and distribution chains.

positions.label: Broad exemption — last-mile distributors qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce, including last-mile delivery workers, are 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — last-mile distributors do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that the Section 1 exemption is limited to workers in the transportation industry who are directly engaged in the movement of goods across borders or state lines, not to workers who merely handle goods that previously traveled in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
